text:
Admission Date:  1979-11-17              Discharge Date:   2008-03-01

Date of Birth:  1991-04-29             Sex:   M

Service: MEDICINE

Allergies:
Patient recorded as having No Known Allergies to Drugs

Attending:Emily
Chief Complaint:
diarrhea, respiratory distress

Major Surgical or Invasive Procedure:
endotracheal intubation

History of Present Illness:
68 y/o M with PMHx significant for COPD, CHF, HTN, C. diff
colitis who presents from home to ED with increased diarrhea x
24hrs. Patient was recently discharged from Fayette Medical Center to Humboldt County Memorial Hospital on
1989-01-20 where he was found to have C. diff colitis and had just
completed course of flagyl. In the ED patient was noted to be
tachypneic and tachycardic. He also had some reported LLQ pain
and underwent CT abdomen which showed mild concentric wall
thickening of decensing colon and sigmoid colon consistent with
either infectious, inflammation or ischemic cause. He was given
dose of levofloxacin and flagyl in the ED. Patient lactate in
the ED was initially 2.0 and then 1.3 upon repeat. In the ED it
was felt that patient maybe getting tired so he was intubated,
his ABG after intubation was 7.29/47/484 then 7.27/47/118. He
underwent a CXR in the ED which was negative for pneumonia,
pulmonary edema, or pneumothorax. Patient got 10mg IV decadron
in the ED. After intubation patient became hypotensive and was
breifly on levophed gtt(approx 45 min), he was then transferred
to th ICU for further management. Of note in the ED cards was
contactLeslie Peters about tachycardia and felt that patient had either MAT
or atrial tach with PAC and recommended holding on BBlocker.

Past Medical History:
- CAD s/p silent IMI; no prior interventions
- Hypercholesterolemia
- Hypertension
- CHF, systolic, EF 30% by echo (Echo 1973-10-13: Overall left
ventricular systolic function is moderately-to-severely
depressed(ejection fraction 30 percent)secondary to severe
hypokinesis of the inferior septum and inferior free wall and
akinesis of the posterior (inferolateral) wall; the rest of the

left ventricle also appears at least mildly hypokinetic)
- COPD
- H/o actinic keratosis s/p cryotherapy 06/2016
- Seborrheic Keratosis
- H/o T1N0M0 NSCLC s/p L superior segmentectomy 09/2001
- New lung nodule on CT of 2002-04-11: surveillance CT in
1980-05-21 identified a new right lower lobe nodule which was

found to be FDG PET avid. He underwent bronchoscopy at the end
of 1979-05-06. The cytology from this examination was
unrevealing, however, the bronchoalveolar lavage grew an
atypical mycobacterium, Mycobacterium kansasii. This is felt to

be a colonizer. Subsequently, he had two induced sputum which
were negative with no growth. Subsequent CT scan in 09
showed decreased size of this RLL lesion. At this point this
lung nodule is felt to be related to aspiration.
- BPH
- Schatzki's Ring
- Dementia not yet fully worked up; per PCP notes pt has been
refusing head imaging to eval for mets
- Silent aspiration per video swallow eval in 04-10
- Recent C. Diff colitis


Social History:
Denies tobacco use; quit smoking 15 yrs ago; There is a history
of alcohol abuse but he states he now abstains. Reports
extensive travel during his life, including many parts of the
southeastern and southwestern US and to East [**Female First Name (un) 8489**]. He was at one
time imprisoned in [**Country 5142**] for dealings in the black market.


Family History:
His father "dropped dead" of a heart attack in his 50's. He was
also a heavy drinker and smoker. To his knowledge, there is no
history of cancer in his family. There are many alcoholics on
both sides of his family. His mother reportedly died of TB.

Physical Exam:
Upon arrival to MICU:
Vitals: T 101 (103 rectal) HR 120 BP 117/78 AC 500x12 FiO2 100
PEEP 5 O2Sat 100%
Gen: Sedated
Heent: ETT in place, OG tube in place; RIJ present
Lungs: Clear and ant and laterally
Cardiac: Irregularly irregular S1/S2 no murmurs
Abdomen: Distended, soft, tympanic, +BS
Ext: no edema,
Neuro: sedated
Skin: intact


Pertinent Results:
1979-11-17 07:15AM   WBC-21.1* RBC-5.27 HGB-15.1 HCT-46.8 MCV-89
MCH-28.7 MCHC-32.4 RDW-14.4
1979-11-17 07:15AM   PLT SMR-NORMAL PLT COUNT-322
1979-11-17 07:15AM   PT-11.1 PTT-19.6* INR(PT)-0.9
1979-11-17 07:15AM   NEUTS-92.3* BANDS-0 LYMPHS-3.8* MONOS-3.6
EOS-0.2 BASOS-0.2
1979-11-17 07:15AM   GLUCOSE-120* UREA N-15 CREAT-1.2 SODIUM-143
POTASSIUM-4.2 CHLORIDE-106 TOTAL CO2-27 ANION GAP-14
1979-11-17 07:15AM   ALT(SGPT)-21 AST(SGOT)-29 CK(CPK)-47 ALK
PHOS-53 AMYLASE-55 TOT BILI-0.8

Micro:
1979-11-17: stool - c dif toxin +

Imaging:
1979-11-17:
CT abd/pelvis:
1. Continuous concentric mild bowel wall thickening from the
splenic flexure distally involving the entire sigmoid colon and
perhaps mildly also the rectum. No evidence of bowel
obstruction, pneumotosis, or portal venous gas. Considerations
would include inflammatory bowel disease such as colitis
ulcerosa or an infectious etiology. Given the distribution of
the bowel wall thickening, an ischemic origin seems less likely.
2. Since 1980-02-23, the left kidney demonstrates new atrophy
and decreased/delayed excretion, likely due to atherosclerotic
involvement of the left renal artery.
3. Cholelithiasis without evidence of cholecystitis.
4. Grossly distended stomach despite NG tube.
.
1979-11-17: CXR: No acute cardiopulmonary process. Tip of central
line now within right brachiocephalic vein.
.
CHEST, ONE VIEW: Comparison with multiple examinations, the most
recent of which is 2007-08-02, at 1333.

Endotracheal tube and right subclavian line are unchanged in
position. Cardiac, mediastinal, and hilar contours are
unchanged. Hazy opacity of the inferior portion of the left
hemithorax is again identified, which may reflect extensive left
lower lobe atelectasis/collapse, pleural fluid, or both. Of the
two, collapse is favored as the descending portion of the left
pulmonary artery appears silhouetted. The right lung is clear.
Both hemidiaphragms appear slightly flattened indicating
emphysema.

IMPRESSION: Hazy opacity over left hemithorax, concerning for
extensive left lower lobe atelectasis/collapse, layering left
pleural effusion, or both.
.
.
Labs on Expiration:
2008-03-01 03:29AM BLOOD WBC-24.5* RBC-3.40* Hgb-10.8* Hct-32.5*
MCV-95 MCH-31.6 MCHC-33.2 RDW-18.2* Plt Ct-90*
2008-03-01 03:29AM BLOOD Glucose-135* UreaN-65* Creat-3.1* Na-139
K-4.3 Cl-106 HCO3-19* AnGap-18
2018-03-12 05:13AM BLOOD ALT-44* AST-40 AlkPhos-293* TotBili-1.0
2008-03-01 03:29AM BLOOD Calcium-9.1 Phos-5.2* Mg-2.1
2008-03-01 05:38AM BLOOD Type-ART pO2-75* pCO2-42 pH-7.20*
calTCO2-17* Base XS--10

Brief Hospital Course:
In brief, the patient is a 68 y/o M with PMHx significant for
COPD, CHF, C. Diff colitis who presents to ED with fevers,
chills, diarrhea complicated by respiratory distress.
.
.
## Colitis/Diarrhea - The patient has a history of recurrent C.
diff colitis and presented with significant diarrhea and fever.
His stools studies were positive for actively shedding c. dif
toxin.  His abdominal imaging was consistent with a diffuse
infectious process.  He was initially treated with Iv flagyl and
oral vancomycin.  Routine stool cultures were sent.  The patient
was transiently hypotensive, but this was felt to be more likely
related to sedating medications peri-intubtation and PEEP than
from sepsis.  His blood pressure rapidly normalized initially.
However, near the end of his life we became increasingly
hypotensive, likely related to his persistent refractory atrial
fibrillation.
.
## Respiratory distress:  The patient's ED course was
complicated by respiratory distress.  There was no evidence of
pneumonia or other acute cardiopulmonary process.  He was
quickly extubated and was breathing well on room air.  The
likely source of his apparent distress was pain from the colitis
which in a patient with such significant COPD was cause for
concern.  His initial steroids directed at a COPD exacerbation
and antibiotics directed at a community acquired pneumonia were
discontinued.  His respiratory status deterioted requiring a
second intubation.  The ICU team had difficulty weaning him off
the ventilator.  He fluctuated bewteen AC and PS ventilation.
Near the end of his life, he would occationally desaturate,
requiring increasingly oxygen requirements and PEEP.  Fluid
given to sustain blood pressure likely contributed to his
decline.  Moreover, he had corresponding hypotension finally
requiring 3 pressors to sustain.  After a meeting with his
health care representative, the decision was made to withdraw
care.  he was thus extubated and passed away shortly thereafter.
 His chief cause of death was cardiopulmonary arrest from
complications related to C. diff, pneumonia/COPD, and refractory
afib.
.
## Non-gap acidosis - Patient ABG consisent with acidosis,
appears to be primary metabolic acidosis that is non-gap, most
likely from diarrhea.  This resolved with hydration and
improvement in his diarrhea.
.
## Tachycardia - The patient's tachycardia was most consistent
with MAT vs atrial tachycardia.  He continued on his home
regimen of beta-blocker and calcium channel blocker.  However,
this was insufficient.  Attempts were made unsuccessfully to
cardiovert him electrically.  EP was consulted for help in
tachycardia management.  Increasing AV nodal blockers were used,
including diltiazem drips.  Amiodarone was also used without
success.  He continued to be in rapid afib/aflutter for the
duration of admission until his death despite maximized medical
therapy.
.
## Renal Failure: Patient suffered non-oliguric ATN requiring
CVVHD during his stay in the MICU.  This was continued until
withdrawing his care.
.
## CHF - Patient with h/o CHF, most recent echo shows LVEF of
30%. Patient did not appear volume overloaded  on presentation
and CXR not consistent with pulmonary edema.  We continued on
his beta-blocker.  Given his hypotension near the end of his
life, the patient was given multiple fluid boluses.
.
## CAD - Patient with hisotry of silent MI, Continued aspirin
and statin.  Patient was ruled out for MI during this admission
by enzymes.
.
## Code: Patient was initially a full code.  However, once his
clinical status deteriorated, he was made comfort measures only
by his wife after extensive discussion and consideration.  He
passed away shortly after withdrawing life support

Medications on Admission:
1. Advair 250/50 Humboldt County Memorial Hospital
2. Albuterol MDI prn
3. Aspirin 325 mg PO DAILY
4. Doxazosin 2 mg qhs
5. Lovastatin 20 mg daily
6. Nitroglycerin 0.3 mg SL as needed
7. Omeprazole 20 mg daily
8. Tiotropium Bromide 1 CAP IH DAILY
9. Toprol XL 100 mg daily
10. Verapamil SR 120 mg daily


Discharge Medications:
